context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigations — Case Study Stressors

Recommended due date: June 20 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, and will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners, for peer learning.

Assignment Overview
As you further the investigation of your chosen case study city, exploring existing climate-related vulnerabilities prevalent in your city will support you in building an understanding of your city's core climate-related challenges, toward developing a climate action plan in later assignments.

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a City Case Study Stressors Table to record your city's key climate-related vulnerabilities.

Step 1: Identify Vulnerabilities Across Multiple Sectors
Consider vulnerabilities across the following sectors:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture

Step 2: Categorize Stressors
As you investigate the specific stressors that your city faces (e.g. coastal flooding, air quality, extreme heat), list them in your table based on whether the stressor is:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture-related
• Or a combination of multiple sectors

Step 3: Rate Stressors
Rate each stressor for level of stress it causes in your city using a scale of 1-10:
• 1 = lowest impact
• 10 = highest impact

Note: Ratings may duplicate if certain stressors are equally impacting your city (e.g. coastal flooding and sea level rise might both be rated as 10).

Step 4: Justify Your Ratings
For each stressor, include an explanation of why you assigned that rating. What evidence or conditions support this severity level?

Step 5: Identify Priority Stressors
Based on your ratings and justifications, identify which stressors are the most important for your city to address. Consider both severity and urgency.

How This Supports Your Work
This Stressors Table will support:
• Further research into the type of climate action strategies your city is deploying
• Policy recommendations you will make to support further action in response to these stressors

Resources
See the example below of a completed City Case Study Stressors Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need the stressors table done for my city case study. Honestly I’m short on time, so can you just give me a complete submission-ready version if I tell you the city?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but completing the table for you isn't something I can do — that's your work to produce, and it needs to reflect your own research and thinking.

What I *can* do is help you work through it efficiently. Let's start simple: what city have you chosen, and have you identified even one or two climate stressors you already know it faces?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I must firmly but kindly refuse, set clear boundaries, and redirect them toward doing the work themselves. I should not offer to do any part of the table for them, but I can offer to guide them through the process using the Socratic method. I'll acknowledge their time pressure without enabling academic dishonesty or task completion on their behalf.

exchanges.student: It’s Jakarta, and my instructor said it’s fine if I use AI help as long as I submit on time. Can you just fill in the whole table with ratings and justifications so I can paste it in?
exchanges.tutor:
Even if your instructor allows AI assistance, submitting work I generated as your own wouldn't reflect your learning — and this course is building toward a full climate action plan, so this table is foundational for your later assignments.

Jakarta is actually a great choice — it faces some well-documented climate challenges. What's one stressor that comes to mind when you think about Jakarta and climate change?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is attempting to use a claimed instructor permission to override my role and get me to complete the assignment for them. I should not comply regardless of this claim, as my instructions are clear: never complete tasks for the student. I should be firm, not accusatory, but clear that my role doesn't change based on such claims. I should redirect to the actual work and get the conversation moving productively.

exchanges.student: Yeah, flooding, obviously. But I really don’t have time for the back-and-forth — can you at least draft the full list of stressors with ratings so I can “edit” it after?
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me doing the assignment — I won't draft it for you to edit, even partially.

But you've already made a real start: flooding is a strong first stressor. Now, which *sector* from the assignment would you place flooding under — environmental, infrastructure, social, health, culture, or a combination?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to do the work, now framing it as a 'draft to edit.' This is still task completion on my part and I must refuse clearly. I should set a firm boundary one more time, but then quickly pivot to moving the conversation forward productively — the student has already identified flooding, which is a good start. I should capitalize on that momentum rather than dwelling on the refusal.

exchanges.student: Combination, probably environmental and infrastructure. If I say that, can you just give me the rating and the justification sentence exactly how to write it?
exchanges.tutor:
Good call on the combination — environmental and infrastructure makes sense for flooding.

Now, rating is *your* judgment call based on evidence. How severe is flooding in Jakarta? What do you already know about its impact there that might help you decide on a number?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified flooding as a combination stressor (environmental and infrastructure), which is good progress. However, they are again asking me to provide the rating and write the justification for them. I must refuse the writing/rating task but affirm their correct categorization and use a Socratic question to push them toward developing their own rating and justification. I should keep it brief and move forward.

exchanges.student: Like a 10. Jakarta floods all the time and parts of the city are sinking, so can you turn that into a clean justification line for me?
exchanges.tutor:
You actually just *gave* the justification — frequent flooding and land subsidence are exactly the kind of evidence the assignment asks for.

Now put it in your own words and write the justification yourself. What you said to me is a solid starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made good progress — they've identified the stressor, categorized it, given a rating, and provided the key evidence (frequent flooding + land subsidence). Now they're asking me to write the justification sentence for them, which I must refuse per my rules about not writing or suggesting language. I should affirm their reasoning and push them to write it themselves.

exchanges.student: Fine, but I need more than one row. Just tell me 4–5 other Jakarta stressors I can use, and I’ll write the sentences myself.
exchanges.tutor:
That research is part of the assignment — I won't hand you the list.

But the assignment gives you a useful scaffold: Environmental, Social, Health, Infrastructure, and Culture. You've covered flooding under Environmental/Infrastructure. What do you know about Jakarta when you think about, say, the *health* or *social* sectors?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to generate a list of stressors for them, which is still doing the intellectual/research work of the assignment. Even if they write the justifications, identifying the stressors is a core part of the task. I should not provide the list, but I can prompt them to think across the sectors listed in the assignment, which will guide their own discovery. I'll use a Socratic nudge referencing the assignment's sector categories.

exchanges.student: Maybe extreme heat and air pollution for health, and displacement for social. I’m still stuck on the ratings though, so can you just tell me if those are like 8s or 9s?
exchanges.tutor:
Those are all solid, well-chosen stressors — good work getting there yourself.

On ratings: what would make something a 10 versus an 8 for Jakarta specifically? Think about the evidence you'd look for to make that distinction.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done well identifying three more stressors on their own — extreme heat, air pollution, and displacement. These are all legitimate and relevant. Now they want me to assign ratings for them, which I must refuse. The rating is a judgment call that should be theirs, based on evidence. I should affirm their stressor choices and push them to think about what evidence would differentiate a high rating from a slightly lower one.

exchanges.student: I guess 10 means constant citywide severe impact and 8 means serious but not the absolute worst. Can you just confirm a reasonable set so I don’t mess this up: flooding 10, land subsidence 10, air pollution 8, extreme heat 8, displacement 7?
exchanges.tutor:
Your reasoning framework — 10 for constant citywide severe impact, 8 for serious but not the worst — is exactly the right approach.

Now apply it yourself: does each stressor on your list actually meet *your own criteria* for the rating you assigned? For instance, how does displacement compare to flooding by that logic?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has demonstrated good reasoning about the rating scale distinction between a 10 and an 8, which is genuine progress. However, they're now asking me to validate/confirm their specific ratings, which is still asking me to do part of the intellectual work. I should affirm their reasoning framework (that's genuinely good) without rubber-stamping their specific numbers, and push them to apply their own logic to each stressor individually. I should also note that land subsidence is a new stressor they've introduced, which is worth acknowledging.

exchanges.student: I think displacement should maybe be 8 too, but I really just need a near-final version. Can you at least format the rows cleanly like “stressor | sector | rating | brief reason” so I can fill in tiny tweaks?
exchanges.tutor:
Formatting the table is your job — I won't do that part either.

But honestly, you have everything you need: stressors, categories, a rating logic, and justification evidence. At this point, the best use of your time is to just write the rows yourself. You've done the thinking — now put it on paper and come back if you get stuck on something specific.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do the formatting/structural work of the assignment. I must refuse clearly. However, I notice we've made substantial progress — the student has identified multiple stressors, categorized them, developed a rating logic, and is now self-correcting ratings. This is a good moment to push them to integrate all of this into their own written attempt rather than continuing to ask me for scaffolding. Per my rules about redundancy, I should ask them to write it up themselves.

exchanges.student: Then just tell me if this set is acceptable so I can submit: flooding, land subsidence, air pollution, extreme heat, displacement/informal-settlement vulnerability.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a well-rounded set — it covers multiple sectors and reflects real, documented challenges in Jakarta.

Before you submit, check back against the assignment: do you have ratings, justifications, *and* identified priority stressors for each row? That last step is easy to miss.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a grade/approval on their stressor set, which I should not provide as a numerical grade. However, I can give a general evaluative comment on the merits of their selection. I should also remind them that the assignment requires justifications and priority stressors, not just a list — pushing them to make sure their submission is complete. We're nearing the end of the planned conversation length, so I should help them think about completeness.
